body:
The Symphony of the Unconscious

I've sat with thousands of people as they've recounted their dreams, and I can tell you that the ones that are often the most potent, the most jarring, are the ones that have a soundtrack. It might be a melody that you've never heard before, a disembodied voice that speaks a profound truth, or even a cacophony of noise that leaves you feeling rattled and disoriented. These auditory experiences in the dreamscape are not random; they are the language of the soul, a direct communication from the deepest parts of your being. We so often focus on the visual spectacle of our dreams, the strange landscapes and the surreal encounters, that we neglect to listen to what our dreams are telling us. The soundscape of your dreams is a rich and fertile ground for self-discovery, a place where the unconscious mind bypasses the filters of the ego and speaks its truth directly. What I've learned after thirty years in this work is that the sounds in our dreams are often the very medicine we need to heal.

Think of it this way: your conscious mind is like a carefully curated playlist, filled with the songs you think you should like, the ones that fit the image you want to project to the world. But your unconscious mind? That’s a sprawling, chaotic, and utterly brilliant music festival, with a thousand different stages playing everything from death metal to devotional chants. And in the middle of it all, there’s you, the dreamer, wandering from stage to stage, drawn to the sounds that resonate with the deepest parts of your being. The music you hear in your dreams is not a passive experience. It is an invitation to dance with the parts of yourself that you have long ignored, to sing along with the voices that you have silenced, and to finally, finally, turn up the volume on your own truth.

In our waking lives, we are constantly bombarded with noise. The traffic, the chatter, the endless notifications on our phones – it all conspires to numb our senses, to disconnect us from the subtle symphony of the universe. We forget how to listen, how to truly hear. But in the dream world, the distractions fall away. The noise of the ego is silenced, and we are able to tune in to a deeper frequency. The sounds we hear in our dreams are a reminder of what we have lost, a call to reclaim our auditory sense as a sacred tool for navigating the world. They are a gateway to a more embodied, more sensual, and more soulful way of being.

If we so desire, we can gently release people, events, beliefs, and circumstances that no longer serve us. In the process, we transform.

When the Dream Sings to You

Music in dreams can be a particularly powerful experience. It can evoke a sense of sublime beauty, a feeling of being connected to something larger than yourself. It can also be a source of deep comfort, a lullaby for the wounded parts of your soul. But don't be fooled by the sweetness of the melody. The music in your dreams is not there to entertain you; it is there to transform you. It is a call to awaken to the music of your own soul, to the unique song that only you can sing. The Swiss psychiatrist Carl Jung, a man who knew a thing or two about the depths of the human psyche, spoke of the "anima" and "animus," the inner feminine and masculine archetypes that guide us on our journey of individuation. When you hear music in your dreams, it is often the voice of your anima or animus, calling you to integrate these powerful energies into your conscious life. The melody is a map, a guide to the hidden treasures of your own being. It is up to you to follow it.

I once sat with a man, a hard-nosed CEO who prided himself on his rationality and logic, who had a recurring dream of a hauntingly beautiful cello melody. He would wake up with tears in his eyes, but he couldn’t understand why. As we worked together, it became clear that the cello was the voice of his repressed feminine side, the part of him that longed for connection, for creativity, for a life that was not just about the bottom line. The dream was calling him to embrace a more soulful way of being, to allow himself to be moved by beauty, to open his heart to the world. It was a long and difficult journey for him, but he eventually learned to listen to the music of his own soul, and in doing so, he became a more whole and integrated human being.

And it’s not just the sweet melodies that have the power to transform. I worked with a young woman, a punk rock musician who was full of rage and rebellion. She had a recurring dream of a distorted, angry guitar riff. She hated the sound, but it wouldn’t leave her alone. As we delved into the dream, she realized that the guitar riff was the sound of her own untamed power, the part of her that she was afraid to fully embrace. She had been told her whole life that her anger was “unladylike,” that she needed to be softer, quieter. The dream was telling her to fuck that. It was telling her to embrace her rage, to turn it into art, to use it as a force for change in the world. She started a new band, and their music was raw, and loud, and utterly brilliant. She had found her voice, and it was a roar.

Love does not require you to be sucked into someone else's void.

We are in control of our thoughts, desires and intentions — creating realities upon every thought and intention.

The Unsettling Voice of Truth

Not all sounds in dreams are pleasant. Sometimes, the dreamscape is filled with a cacophony of noise, a jarring and dissonant soundtrack that leaves you feeling anxious and overwhelmed. This is not a sign that there is something wrong with you. It is a sign that you are finally starting to listen. The noise is the sound of your own repressed emotions, the parts of yourself that you have deemed unacceptable and pushed into the shadows. The anger, the grief, the fear – they are all clamoring for your attention, demanding to be heard. The dream is giving you an opportunity to face these parts of yourself, to integrate them into your being, and to reclaim the power that you have given away. It is a messy and uncomfortable process, but it is the only way to true freedom. You cannot bypass the darkness and expect to find the light. You must be willing to sit in the fire of your own uncomfortable truths.

I remember a woman who came to me, a spiritual seeker who had spent years cultivating a persona of love and light. She had a recurring dream of a baby crying, a sound that filled her with a sense of dread and revulsion. She had convinced herself that the baby represented her own immaturity, her own neediness, and she was trying to "transcend" it. But as we explored the dream, it became clear that the baby was the voice of her own wounded inner child, the part of her that had been neglected and abandoned. The dream was not asking her to transcend her pain; it was asking her to embrace it, to hold it, to love it back to wholeness. It was a radical act of self-acceptance, and it was the beginning of her true healing journey.

The shadow, as Jung called it, is the container for all the parts of ourselves that we have disowned. It is the repository of our shame, our guilt, our rage, our grief. And it is also the source of our greatest power. When we have the courage to turn towards the unsettling sounds in our dreams, we are turning towards our own shadow. We are saying, “I am willing to see all of myself, not just the pretty parts.” This is the work of the spiritual warrior. It is the work of the alchemist, who transforms the lead of our pain into the gold of our wisdom. It is not easy, but it is the only work that matters.

If they've got a tattoo of a genderless inner child floating in a pink bubble… run.

The past does not exist. It is a cancelled check, a forgotten wind, a story I created to feed my ego.

The Sound of Silence

Sometimes, the most profound sound in a dream is the sound of silence. It is a silence that is not empty, but full. It is a silence that is pregnant with meaning, a silence that speaks volumes. This is the silence of the void, the space between thoughts, the place where all creation begins. When you experience this kind of silence in a dream, it is a sign that you are being invited to let go of the noise of your mind and to rest in the stillness of your own being. It is a call to surrender, to release your grip on control, and to trust in the wisdom of the universe. This is not a passive act. It is an act of radical courage. It is the willingness to be undone, to be dissolved, to be remade in the image of your own true nature. It is in the silence that you will find your true voice, the voice that has been waiting to be heard all along.

A young artist I worked with was plagued by creative blocks. He was constantly striving, pushing, trying to force his art into existence. He had a dream where he was standing in a vast, empty desert, and the only sound was the sound of his own breathing. In the dream, he was terrified at first, but then a sense of profound peace washed over him. He realized that he didn’t have to create anything, that he could simply be. When he woke up, he started to approach his art in a new way. He would sit in silence for long periods of time, simply listening, waiting for the inspiration to arise. And it always did. He learned that true creativity is not about forcing, but about receiving. It is about getting out of your own way and allowing the universe to create through you.

You weren't wrong. You were just open and naive — in a system built on bullshit.

There is a difference between the empty silence of disconnection and the full silence of presence. The empty silence is the silence of the tomb, the silence of a life unlived. The full silence is the silence of the womb, the silence of a life about to be born. In our culture, we are terrified of silence. We fill every moment with noise, with distraction, with busy-ness. But it is only in the silence that we can hear the voice of our own soul. It is only in the silence that we can connect with the deep, abiding wisdom that is our birthright. So, I invite you to cultivate silence in your life. Turn off the TV. Put down your phone. Go for a walk in nature. Sit in meditation. And just listen. Listen to the silence. It has so much to teach you.

What Changes When Sound Lands

When you begin to pay attention to the soundscape of your dreams, everything changes. You start to see the world with new eyes, to hear with new ears. You become more attuned to the subtle energies that are constantly flowing around you. You start to recognize the voice of your intuition, the quiet whisper of your soul that is always guiding you towards your highest good. You become more discerning, more able to distinguish between the voice of truth and the voice of fear. You start to trust yourself more, to rely on your own inner wisdom instead of seeking validation from outside sources. You become a more powerful creator, more able to bring your desires and to bring your dreams into reality. This is not a game. This is the real work of transformation. It is the work of a lifetime, and it is the most important work you will ever do.

This is not about becoming some kind of "dream whisperer" or developing a new spiritual superpower. It is about becoming more fully human. It is about reclaiming the parts of yourself that you have lost, and integrating them into a more whole and authentic expression of who you are. It is about learning to live with more courage, more compassion, and more creativity. It is about waking up to the symphony of your own life, and finally, finally, daring to conduct it.

So how do you begin this work? You start by paying attention. When you wake up from a dream, don’t just focus on the plot. Ask yourself: What did I hear? Was there music? A voice? A strange noise? Write it down in your dream journal. Don’t try to interpret it right away. Just record it. Then, as you go about your day, notice the sounds around you. What do you hear? What do you tune out? Start to cultivate a more conscious relationship with the world of sound. The more you listen to the world around you, the more you will be able to listen to the world within you.

Don't be a carnival barker giving out cheap trinkets and toys.

Find quality people and get to know them personally. Pay them what they're worth, then give them the support they'll need to hit home runs.

Frequently Asked Questions

What does it mean if I can't remember the music in my dream?

Even if you can't recall the specific melody, the feeling that the music evoked is the most important piece of information. Did it make you feel joyful, sad, or anxious? The emotion is the key to unlocking the dream's message. The specifics of the music are less important than the emotional residue it leaves behind. Trust the feeling. Don't get hung up on the details. The unconscious speaks in the language of emotion, not in the language of logic. The feeling is the truth.

Are there certain sounds that have universal meanings in dreams?

While some sounds may have archetypal associations, the meaning of any sound in a dream is ultimately personal. A ringing phone might symbolize an opportunity for one person, and a source of anxiety for another. The key is to explore your own personal associations with the sound and to ask yourself what it means to you. Don't rely on generic dream dictionaries. They are the fast food of the spiritual world – cheap, easy, and ultimately unsatisfying. Do the real work of exploring your own inner landscape.

What if the sounds in my dreams are frightening?

Frightening sounds in dreams are often a sign that you are confronting a part of yourself that you have been avoiding. While it can be unsettling, it is actually a positive sign. It means you are ready to face your fears and to integrate the shadow aspects of your personality. The dream is providing a safe container for you to do this work. Don't run from the noise. Turn towards it. Ask it what it wants. What it needs. You might be surprised by the answer.

Can I learn to control the sounds in my dreams?

While lucid dreaming techniques can allow you to have some influence over your dreams, the goal is not to control the sounds, but to understand them. The sounds are a message from your unconscious, and your job is to listen, not to dictate. The more you try to control your dreams, the more you will miss the wisdom they have to offer. Let go of the need to be in charge. Surrender to the mystery. The dream knows what it’s doing, even if you don’t.

How can I better remember the sounds in my dreams?

Keep a dream journal by your bed and write down everything you can remember as soon as you wake up. Don't just focus on the visual elements; make a conscious effort to recall any sounds you heard. The more you practice, the better you will become at remembering the rich and detailed soundscapes of your dreams. And don’t just write about the sounds. Draw them. Paint them. Dance them. Find a way to give them form in the waking world. This will help you to integrate their wisdom and to make them a part of your conscious life.

Don't just read this. Use it. Fight with it. Let it change you.

So, what are you going to do with this? Are you going to continue to ignore the soundtrack of your soul, or are you going to finally start listening? The choice is yours. The music is playing. Are you ready to dance?

faqs.question: How can I work with these dreams practically?
faqs.answer: Start by writing the dream down within five minutes of waking. Do not interpret — describe. Then sit with the images for a day before you try to make meaning. The dream will reveal itself on its own schedule, not yours.

faqs.question: Can two people share the same dream experience?
faqs.answer: Shared dreaming is reported across cultures and throughout history. Whether you frame it as telepathy, collective unconscious, or coincidence depends on your worldview. What matters is what the shared dream is communicating to each person individually.
